coredata.dc:title: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots prepared using high-softening point asphalt and their application in Fe3+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ruyi
Fan, Huailin
Jiang, Wei
Ni, Guosong
Qu, Shijie
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are known to display tunable photoluminescence (PL) proprieties by functional modification. Here, yellow fluorescence amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) were synthesized using high-softening point asphalt as precursor and ammonia as nitrogen source, and the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement by nitrogen-containing functional groups is also discussed in detail. The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the resultant GQDs increased from 4.7% to 13.8% after the amino-hydrothermal treatment. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the af-GQDs have more uniform size distribution than GQDs. PL and ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–Vis) suggested that two types of electron transitions namely π-π∗ and n-π∗ occurred intrinsically in GQDs, and n-π∗ transition of functional groups was more prominent in af-GQDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses demonstrated that reduction reactions occurred on the surface of GQDs, and nitrogen functional groups were introduced into GQDs during the amino-hydrothermal treatment process. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) results confirmed that the energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbit increased from 6.98 to 7.26 eV. The amide and amino groups were considered to contribute to the fluorescence enhancement of GQDs. Furthermore, the af-GQDs were used as effective fluorescent sensing probes to detect Fe3+ ions, which have a high sensitivity, good selectivity, and superior detection limit of 0.51 nM, indicating their promising application in trace Fe3+ ion detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
High-softening point asphalt
Amino functionalized
N-doping
Fe3+ detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of the underlying mechanism for fluorescence enhancement
Fe3+ detection using af-GQDs as the fluorescent probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The af-GQD powder and (b) fluorescence contrast pictures of af-GQDs (left) and GQDs (right) of the same concentration (digital images were recorded under irradiation of 365 nm UV light and daylight).
Emission spectra of GQDs solutions neutralized with different reagents before and after the hydrothermal treatment.

TEM images of GQDs (a) and af-GQDs (b); the upper left and right insets show particle size distribution and HRTEM images of obtained GQDs, respectively. AFM images of GQDs (c) and af-GQDs (d), height profiles of GQDs (e) and af-GQDs (f), and height distribution of GQDs (g) and af-GQDs (h).
Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs (a) and af-GQDs (b) in aqueous solution under shifted excitation wavelengths. The mass concentrations of GQDs and af-GQDs were both 0.1 mg/mL.
UV–vis absorption and PL excitation spectra of GQDs and af-GQDs.
Raman spectra of GQDs and af-GQDs.
FT-IR spectra of prepared GQDs and af-GQDs.
TGA/DTG curves of GQDs (a) and af-GQDs (b) measured in high-purity argon atmosphere.
UPS spectra of GQDs and af-GQDs.
(a) XPS full-scan spectra, high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b) and N1s (d) of GQDs, C1s (c) and N1s (e) of GQDs and af-GQDs, respectively.

Relative fluorescence intensity of af-GQD and GQD solutions in the presence of different metal ions ([Mn+] = 100 μM; F0 and F values were recorded fluorescence intensities at λem = 500 nm and λex = 450 nm, respectively).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs in PBS solution (pH = 7.4) at different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–100 μM (excitation at 450 nm, af-GQD concentration 0.05 mg/mL). (b) Linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentrations varying from 1 to 50 μM (fluorescence intensities at 500 nm in the presence of Fe3+).
Relative fluorescence intensity of the af-GQD solution in the presence of Fe3+ and Fe3+ co-existing with other different metal ions ([Fe3+] = 30 μM, [Mn+] = 300 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs in the Fen river water samples in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 0–130 μM (excitation at 450 nm, af-GQDs concentration 0.05 mg/mL). (b) Linear relationship between the F0/F ratio and Fe3+ concentration varying from 1 to 90 μM (fluorescence intensities at 500 nm in the presence of Fe3+).
Ultimate analysis results of GQDs and af-GQDs.
Comparison of different types of Fe3+ ion fluorescence probes.

row.entry: Turn-on fluorescent sensor; high specificity; complex process; high cost
Turn-off fluorescent sensor; low specificity; Interference: Hg(II); expensive precursor
Turn-off fluorescent sensor; high specificity; cytotoxicity; high cost
Turn-off fluorescent sensor; high specificity; complex process; low cost
Turn-off fluorescent sensor; high specificity; easy process; nontoxicity; low cost

head.ce:title: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots prepared using high-softening point asphalt and their application in Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high temperature asphalt was preoxidized and carbonized before acid oxidation to prepared graphene quantum dots. Stepwise, the graphene quantum dots was neutralized with ammonia and hydrothermal treated. The as-synthesized graphene quantum dots can emit bright yellow fluorescence, and it have fluorescent quenching effect while mixed with Fe3+ solution, hence, it was applied as an effective fluorescent sensing probe to detect Fe3+ ions.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are known to display tunable photoluminescence (PL) proprieties by functional modification. Here, yellow fluorescence amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) were synthesized using high-softening point asphalt as precursor and ammonia as nitrogen source, and the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement by nitrogen-containing functional groups is also discussed in detail. The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the resultant GQDs increased from 4.7% to 13.8% after the amino-hydrothermal treatment. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the af-GQDs have more uniform size distribution than GQDs. PL and ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–Vis) suggested that two types of electron transitions namely π-π∗ and n-π∗ occurred intrinsically in GQDs, and n-π∗ transition of functional groups was more prominent in af-GQDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses demonstrated that reduction reactions occurred on the surface of GQDs, and nitrogen functional groups were introduced into GQDs during the amino-hydrothermal treatment process. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) results confirmed that the energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbit increased from 6.98 to 7.26 eV. The amide and amino groups were considered to contribute to the fluorescence enhancement of GQDs. Furthermore, the af-GQDs were used as effective fluorescent sensing probes to detect Fe3+ ions, which have a high sensitivity, good selectivity, and superior detection limit of 0.51 nM, indicating their promising application in trace Fe3+ ion detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the newest members of the graphene family, have attracted considerable attention since their discovery. As GQDs are zero-dimensional graphene materials, they have many unique and excellent physical and chemical properties, such as water solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, chemical inertness, and strong photoluminescence (PL) emission. Therefore, various GQDs have been synthesized and widely used in applications such as fluorescent probing [1,2] [Dong, 2017 #2] [Dong, 2017 #2], bioimaging [3,4], drug delivery [5], photocatalysis [6], supercapacitors [7], and solar cells [8]. To improve the performance of GQDs and expand their application fields, they are commonly functionalized by surface modification and heteroatom doping [9–12]. Heteroatom doping can change the π-conjugated system or the surface state of GQDs, thereby providing more active sites and innovative properties of GQDs for new applications. Nitrogen doping is the most used method to modify GQDs properties because the atomic radius of nitrogen is close to that of carbon and because it has five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms [13,14]. Nitrogen doping is usually accompanied by an increase in the fluorescence quantum yield (QY), which is related to the change in energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO) levels. Herein, amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs), which have excellent fluorescence performance, were successfully synthesized with ammonia as nitrogen source and asphalt as carbon source.

It is well known that precursors play a critical role in the fluorescence property of GQDs, and it is considered that precursors containing abundant sp2 carbon domains are more suitable for preparing GQDs. Therefore, carbon sources such as graphene [15], carbon nanotubes [16], carbon fibers [17], carbon nano-onions [18], and coal [19] were selected to synthesize GQDs. However, these materials are usually expensive or have a high added value; therefore, it is imperative to develop an efficient way for the preparation of high-performance GQDs with a cheap polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon source. Asphalt, which is a byproduct of coking, is considered to be an ideal source to prepare GQDs because of its high carbon content and aromaticity and rich source. High-softening point asphalt is a mixture of complex chemical compositions, which is rich in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [20]. It contains microstructure unit similar to graphene, and it can be easily cut into small pieces and modified. Theoretically, it is a facile approach to synthesize GQDs from high-softening point asphalts.

In this work, the method of pre-oxidization and carbonization of high-softening point asphalt to prepare GQDs was first proposed, and the amino-modified GQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation and amino-hydrothermal treatment. The as-synthesized af-GQDs were highly homogeneous with particle size less than 4 nm and showed an uncommon yellow fluorescence. The af-GQDs had a fluorescence intensity and QY higher than GQDs (Fig. 1 ), and the underlying mechanism for the observed results was discussed comprehensively. In addition, the af-GQDs as fluorescent probe showed high sensitivity and selectivity in Fe3+ detection. This article provides a facile method to obtain af-GQDs and offers an efficient tool for trace Fe3+ ion detection.

The high-softening point asphalt was purchased from Jining Carbon Group Co. Sulfuric acid, nitric acid, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co Ltd. All inorganic salts, namely LiCl2, NaCl2, KNO3, AgNO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, Zn(NO3)2, FeCl2, Cu(NO3)2, MnCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, FeCl3, AlCl3, and CrCl3, used in the experiment were of analytical grade. Deionized water was used in all tests without special instructions. River water samples were obtained from the Fen River of Taiyuan, Shanxi Province, China.

The high-softening point asphalt was first preoxidized under air at 320 °C for 4 h and then carbonized under helium at 900 °C for 1 h; 1 g carbonized asphalt was added into a mixture of 40 mL sulfuric acid and 20 mL nitric acid, and the mixed solution was treated by ultrasonication for 1 h. Then, the solution was heated to 100 °C in an oil bath and refluxed for 23 h. The solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the solution was diluted with water to 500 mL, and ammonia was used to adjust the pH to 7. Finally, the solution was treated in two steps. In the first step, the neutralized GQDs solution was dialyzed directly, and in the second step, the neutralized solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a homogeneous reactor, and then dialyzed. The GQDs solution was dialyzed using a membrane (3500 MWCO) for 3 days until nitrates and sulfates were removed, and a yellow solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying to evaporate water. The yields of GQDs and af-GQDs are presented in Table S1. In the following test, the lyophilized samples were re-dissolved in deionized water to obtain the desired solutions.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100F electron microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed using a Bruker Dimension Edge. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Bruker D8 diffractometer with high-intensity Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) from 4° to 90°. Ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using an UNICO UV visible spectrophotometer UV-2102c with a scanning range of 190–1000 nm. PL emission and excitation spectra were measured at room temperature using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were obtained using a Renishaw 1000 microspectrometer equipped with a 514.5 nm Ar+ ion laser excitation source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterizations of the samples were analyzed using a Bruker TENSOR II FT-IR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a Setsys Evolution SETARAM thermal analyzer by heating the sample under argon flow to 900 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was performed with Thermo Fisher 250-xi using a He (I) laser source. XPS spectra were measured using an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Al-Kɑ radiation. Ultimate analysis was performed using a vario EL CUBE elemental analyzer.

All measurements were made for GQDs solution with the concentration of 0.05 mg/mL in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH = 7.4 without special instructions. All metal salt solutions were dissolved in deionized water. These solutions were mixed with 2 mL GQDs solution for 2 min and then analyzed under an excitation of 450 nm. To evaluate the practicality of the af-GQDs fluorescence probe, the water sample collected from the Fen River, Taiyuan City, was tested using the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the underlying mechanism for fluorescence enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: First, the high-softening point asphalt was directly used to prepare GQDs, but almost no fluorescence was observed in the obtained solution. However, after preoxidizing and carbonizing asphalt, a GQDs solution emitting ordinary yellow fluorescence was obtained by chemical oxidation. Interestingly, when ammonia-neutralized GQDs solution was hydrothermally treated, the fluorescence intensity enhanced by approximately five times. It should be noted that ammonia was a key factor in neutralizing the GQDs solution before hydrothermal treatment, whereas NaOH, Na2CO3, and NaHCO3 did not provide a satisfactory result. The emission spectra at 315 nm excitation of GQDs solutions of the same concentration but neutralized with different reagents are shown in Fig. 2 , which show that NH3·H2O plays a considerable role in fluorescence enhancement. The schematic illustration of preoxidation and carbonization of high-softening point asphalt is shown in Fig. 3 . The pretreatment of high-softening point asphalt was crucial because preoxidation resulted in the crosslinking of partly cyclic asphalt molecules to fix the molecular structure, and carbonization improved the degree of molecular aromatization and purified the constituents. Furthermore, the pretreatment of asphalt could enhance the yield of GQDs tremendously. The treated asphalt possessed a more aromatic structure, and the fraction of sp2- to sp3-hybridized carbon atoms was significantly enhanced. Asphalt containing a large amount of small sp2 carbon domains would be easily exfoliated and surface functionalized by chemical oxidation to form GQDs. It was reported that the high content of sp2 carbon domains could lower the π-π* transition energy gap and further lead to an easier electron transfer in the surface state [21]. To clearly study the difference in morphological and structural changes between GQDs and af-GQDs, a series of characterizations were performed.

Fig. 4 (a) and (b) show the TEM images of GQDs and af-GQDs, respectively. The images showed that the average particle size of af-GQDs (2.3 nm) was smaller than that of GQDs (5.2 nm). However, it was found from PL analysis that this was not the reason for fluorescence enhancement. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of GQDs and af-GQDs well proved the existence of the crystalline structure. The lattice distances belonged to the (0 0 2) interlayer spacing between graphene layers [22,23]; furthermore, the broad peak centered at approximately 26° in the XRD spectrum also confirmed the graphene (0 0 2) face of af-GQDs (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). By performing fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis, the lattice distances were found to be 0.344 and 0.338 nm for GQDs and af-GQDs, respectively; these values are close to that of the graphene bulk (0.334 nm). The small interlayer spacing could be attributed to the fact that oxygen-containing groups between graphene layers were partly removed after the amino-hydrothermal treatment, and the small graphene microcrystals with few structural defects had an effective π-π stacking [13,24]. The corresponding AFM images of GQDs and af-GQDs (Fig. 4 (c)–(h)) revealed that the majority of GQDs and af-GQDs had a thickness less than 2 nm, indicating that they had 1–3 graphene layers.

Fig. 7 shows the Raman spectra of GQDs and af-GQD. Characteristic D and G bands emerged at 1330 and 1570 cm−1, respectively. The presence of D peak related to the presence of sp3 defects of oxygen functional groups in the carbon network, and the crystalline (G) band related to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon. In this work, the ID/IG ratios of GQDs and af-GQDs were both 1.2, demonstrating that the amino-hydrothermal treatment caused negligible alteration in the graphitic structures of GQDs. Furthermore, the high intensity ratio ID/IG indicated that the small crystallite size and abundant disordered edges resulted from the acid exfoliation [30].

From the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 8 , the broad absorption peaks at 3409 and 3153 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively. The peak at 1699 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of CO, and the wide peak at 1600 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CC. The peak at 1338 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of the CN bond, and the characteristic bands at approximately 900 cm−1 was due to the wagging vibration of NH2, which was obviously observed in af-GQDs, indicating that nitrogen-containing functional groups were successfully linked to the GQD structure. The presence of peaks at 1699, 1338, and 900 cm−1 demonstrated the existence of primary amide group (CONH2), and the formation mechanism can be ascribed to the reaction of amino group with adjacent carboxyl groups [31]. The presence of an amide or amino group had a certain passivation effect on the surface of GQDs, and it is speculated that amination and amidation changed the whole electronic system and the molecular orbital distribution in GQDs, as shown in Fig. 9 . Furthermore, the lone pair electrons of nitrogen atoms could increase the HOMO level and lower the energy gap, thus enabling more effective radiative recombination of electrons and holes and further improving the luminous efficiency of GQDs, a similar result has been reported earlier [32,33]. The weak peak at 1252 cm−1 was due to the vibration of epoxy group (COC), which almost vanished after the amino-hydrothermal treatment; this indicates that deoxidization occurred during the process. Thus, the changes in functional groups indicate that the amino-hydrothermal treatment introduced nitrogen functional groups into GQDs, while it removed some oxygen atoms. It had been proposed that the oxygen groups linked to graphene nanosheets could be removed partly at high temperatures, thereby providing active sites for nitrogen doping into graphene frameworks [34,35]. Carboxyl and epoxy groups can act as nonradiative centers for electron-hole recombination; therefore, the fluorescence QY may be improved by removing oxygen-containing groups.

The thermal decompositions of GQDs and af-GQDs were evaluated by TGA (Fig. 10 ). The initial weight loss at 100 °C was due to the evaporation of water molecules. The multiple weight loss peaks of derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) observed in the range of 150 to 600 °C were attributed to the decomposition of surface functional groups and the peaks above 600 °C were due to the decomposition of the carbon backbone. A comparison of the decomposition curves of GQDs and af-GQDs, shown in Fig. 10(a) and (b), respectively, showed that the loss peaks at 400 °C and 550 °C were different, which can be explained due to the changes in different surface functional groups, such as amide and amino groups. In conclusion, the intrinsic structures of GQDs and af-GQDs were different.

UPS was performed to calculate the HOMO levels of GQDs and af-GQDs (Fig. 11 ). The valence band onset (Eonset) and secondary electron cutoff (Ecutoff) of GQDs and af-GQDs are shown in Supporting Information Fig. S2, which were estimated to be 3.30 and 17.54 eV for GQDs and 3.55 and 17.51 eV for af-GQDs, respectively. The valence band maxima (or HOMO) can be calculated from the following equation:
HOMO(eV) = h ν - (
E cutoff
-
E onset
)

The incident energy was from a He1 laser source (hν = 21.22 eV), and the HOMO levels were calculated to be 6.98 and 7.26 eV for GQDs and af-GQDs, respectively. Apparently, this result was well consistent with the assumption that the amino-hydrothermal process enhanced the HOMO level of GQDs. The higher HOMO level further triggered a lower electron transition energy, which led to a more effective radiative recombination of electrons and holes.

The XPS full-scan spectra of obtained GQDs before and after the amino-hydrothermal treatment are presented in Fig. 12 (a), which demonstrates the presence of three main elements, namely C, N, and O, in the composite of GQDs. The presence of nitrogen element before the amino-hydrothermal treatment originated from asphalt and HNO3 oxidation in the process of GQD preparation. To further clarify the existing forms of carbon and nitrogen elements, detailed C 1s and N 1s XPS scans were performed. The C 1s (Fig. 12(b) and (c)) spectra were fitted with four binding energy peaks at approximately 284.7, 285.2, 286.1, and 288.5 eV, which can be assigned to the sp2 bonds namely CC/CC, CN/CN, CO, and CO, respectively. By comparison, the high-resolution spectra of C 1s represent that the chemical environment of carbon in GQDs has changed significantly before and after the amino-hydrothermal treatment. The number of CN and CN bonds obviously increased, while the number of CO and CO bonds clearly decreased after the amino-hydrothermal treatment, which were highly consistent with the FT-IR results and further confirmed the assumption of CO removal and nitrogen element doping. The amino-hydrothermal treatment increased the contribution of CN bonds at the expense of consuming oxygen groups [36]. The high-resolution spectra of N 1s, shown in Fig. 12(d) and (e), showed completely different existing forms of nitrogen, indicating that the conformation of nitrogen functional groups was greatly changed by the amino-hydrothermal treatment. The new binding energy peaks at approximately 399.1 and 401.4 eV are assigned to the amino N and amide N [35], which demonstrate that amination and amidation reactions occurred during the amino-hydrothermal treatment. A high binding energy peak was found at 406.1 eV before the amino-hydrothermal treatment, and it almost vanished after the treatment. This is because of the formation of aromatic nitro-compounds during the acid oxidation process and reduction during the amino-hydrothermal treatment. The ultimate analysis results of GQDs and af-GQDs are shown in Table 1 . The N/C ratio increased from 0.12 to 0.21 after the amino-hydrothermal treatment; this coincides with the results of XPS analysis. The lower carbon content in af-GQDs was ascribed to the smaller size and abundant surface functional groups in af-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection using af-GQDs as the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ ions are known to play vital roles in biological systems and ecological environment. On the one hand, Fe3+ ions can be involved in various physiological processes in the body; however, excess Fe3+ ions will cause cell oxidation and threaten human health, for example, high Fe3+ concentration is an important influencing factor for Parkinson’s disease [37]. Moreover, the content of heavy metal ions is also an important index for water quality monitoring. Excessive iron ions will cause water pollution and damage the environment. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ concentration is of vital importance both in biological systems and environmental monitoring. The prepared af-GQDs were used to detect various metal ions to study the selectivity toward different metal ions, including Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, and Cr3+. The PL response to different metal ions with the same Fe3+ concentration (100 μM) in af-GQD and GQD solution (0.05 mg/mL) is shown in Fig. 13 . It can be seen that Fe3+ ions had the most obvious fluorescence quenching effect; furthermore, the sensitivity and selectivity of GQDswere lower than those of af-GQDs in Fe3+ detection. Electron transfer is known to be a major pathway to quench the fluorescence of the fluorescent probe. As transition metal ions, Fe3+ ions are unstable in performance and easily combine with different ligands as electron acceptors. The high selectivity of af-GQDs toward Fe3+ may be due to the high thermodynamic affinity of GQDs and a higher chelating speed of Fe3+ than those of other introduced ions [38,39]. Therefore, a coordinated Fe/af-GQD complex may be formed, which will promote charge transfer and inhibit exciton recombination, thus leading to PL quenching behavior. In addition, related literature reported that Fe3+ ions display a high affinity to the O and N elements on the surface of GQDs; herein, abundant OH, COOH, and NH2 groups existed on the surfaces, which will also greatly accelerate aggregation to form Fe/af-GQD chelates, thus triggering fluorescence quenching [40–42].

In addition, the incubation time of the sensing system was also investigated. As shown in Supporting Information Fig. S3, the time dependence of fluorescence response upon the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ at room temperature was studied. The fluorescence intensity declined rapidly and was almost constant after 2 min; this indicates that af-GQDs have an efficient response speed to Fe3+ ions. Therefore, no additional time is required to incubate, and the below operations of ions detection are all finished in 2 min. Moreover, the pH-dependent PL spectra were also recorded (Supporting Information Fig. S4); it can be seen that pH has an important influence on the PL emission of af-GQDs. When the pH is increased from 2 to 13, the PL intensity increased first and then decreased, thereby presenting a strongest intensity at pH = 7; this indicates that the optimal working condition of af-GQDs is neutral. To further assess the prospective of af-GQDs for detecting Fe3+ in aqueous solutions, the fluorescence quenching curves of af-GQDs obtained at different Fe3+ ion concentrations varying from 1 to 100 μM are shown in Fig. 14 (a). The PL spectra at various Fe3+ ion concentrations were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm in a PBS solution (pH = 7.4). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs gradually decreased with the increase in Fe3+ ion concentration. As shown in Fig. 14(b), the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 0–50 μM demonstrated a good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9956. The fluorescence quenching statistics of af-GQDs toward Fe3+ were evaluated by the following correlation Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
c
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of af-GQDs at λex/λem of 450/500 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant of Fe3+, and c is the Fe3+ ion concentration. The resultant linear relationship can be described as F0/F = 1.0911 + 0.03032·c (μM), and the limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ was estimated to be 0.51 nM (signal-to-noise S/N = 3) based on 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of blank sample (n = 11). This LOD is much lower than that reported by many articles, as shown in Table 2 . To examine the anti-interference performance of af-GQDs, we also tested the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs in the presence of 30 μM Fe3+ and 50-fold concentration of Na+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Al3+, and Cu2+ interferential metal ions (Fig. 15 ). It can be clearly seen that the addition of interfering ions has no obvious effect on the fluorescence quenching degree. The results indicated that a new fluorescence sensing platform based on af-GQDs can detect Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. The reversible bonding between af-GQDs and Fe3+ was investigated by adding ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) into the solution (Supporting Information Fig. S5(a)). The intensity of PL was recovered when EDTA was added to the af-GQDs/Fe3+ system, and the PL was quenched again while increasing the Fe3+ concentration. This confirmed that the chelation of Fe3+ and af-GQD could be broken by the EDTA solution. In other words, Fe3+ ions could be released gradually from af-GQDs/Fe3+ in the presence of EDTA, and the time-dependent PL change of the recovery system was also investigated (Supporting Information Fig. S5(b)).

To evaluate the efficiency of af-GQDs in Fe3+ detection in real water samples, samples were collected from the Fen River, Taiyuan City, Shan Xi Province, China (Fig. 16 ). The river water samples were simply filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane without any additional process. Although there were numerous organic matters, inorganic salts, microorganisms, and other impurities in the river water samples, the fluorescence quenching spectra of af-GQDs still presented an excellent performance with a change pattern similar to that observed for PBS solution. It was noted that the response degree decreased slightly for river water samples, which could be explained by the fact that the organic matters or impurities in the water samples hindered the binding of Fe3+ to af-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase in Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 0–130 μM, and a good linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 0–90 μM with a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9937. The resultant linear relationship can be described as F0/F = 1.0126 + 0.015·c (μM). The values of recovery measured by af-GQDs were within the range of 95–105% for both PBS solution and river water (Supporting Information Table S2). The accuracy and reliability of af-GQDs as fluorescent probes indicate their enormous potential in practical applications.

In summary, af-GQDs with yellow photoluminescence were successfully synthesized from modified high-softening point asphalt using an efficient route by combining chemical and amino-hydrothermal methods. The method of treating asphalt is novel, which can not only improve the yield of GQDs but also increase the fluorescence QY. The amino-hydrothermal process greatly enhanced the fluorescence QY from 4.7% of GQDs to 13.8% of af-GQDs. The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement was analyzed by adequate characterization methods. The formation of amino and amide contributes to the n-π* transition in GQDs, which was more prominent in the long-wavelength excitation range 400–500 nm. By investigating the electronic energy levels, it can be concluded that the nitrogen lone pair electrons increased the HOMO level and decreased the transition energy, thus enabling a more effective radiative recombination of electrons and holes and further improvement in the fluorescence QY. The af-GQDs were also demonstrated as an effective fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ ions with a superior detection limit of 0.51 nM; moreover, there is no incubation process because of the rapid response of af-GQDs to Fe3+. The real detection in river water also showed a promising application in trace Fe3+ ion detection in waste water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFE0203500) and the National Science Foundation of China (No. U1510122).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.10.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
